topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum with accusation. Liam stood by his duffel bag, his knuckles white where he gripped the worn leather. Coach Miller’s voice, usually booming with encouragement, was low and measured, each word landing like a carefully placed stone. "Liam, we need to be realistic about your role moving forward." A tightness coiled in Liam’s chest, a physical constriction that made drawing a full breath feel like a conscious effort. He looked down at his cleats, tracing the faded stitching with a restless finger. He could feel the heat rising in his neck, a flush creeping up his jawline. He wanted to argue, to defend the hours he’d poured into this team, but the words caught in his throat, thick and useless. When the coach finally delivered the decision, it wasn't a shout, but a quiet finality that felt heavier than any reprimand. Liam nodded once, his gaze fixed on the floor, the sound of his own breathing suddenly deafening in the silence.

[story 2]

I kept my eyes fixed on the scuff marks on the polished floor, willing myself to remain still. Every time Coach Davies addressed me, I felt a strange lightness, as if my bones were vibrating just beneath the surface. His tone was clinical, detached, discussing statistics and team dynamics as if I were a variable in an equation that had just been solved. My hands, tucked deep into my pockets, were clammy. I could feel the prickle of tears gathering behind my eyes, hot and unwelcome. "Your skill set isn't aligning with the current strategy, Mark." The dismissal was so smooth, so impersonal, it scraped against something deep inside me. I managed a small, jerky nod, the movement feeling alien. I wanted to push back, to articulate the frustration bubbling up—the sheer unfairness of it all—but all that escaped was a shallow, shaky exhale.

[story 3]

The air in the gym felt thin, stretched taut between the coach and me. When Coach Reynolds finally spoke, his voice carried a weary resignation that settled over the room like a heavy blanket. He didn't look directly at me; instead, he stared at the whiteboard, his pen hovering over a line of notes. I could feel a distinct pulling sensation in my stomach, a sickening lurch that made my knees feel weak. It wasn't anger I felt, not exactly; it was a profound sense of being overlooked, of having been misread entirely. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action difficult. "This isn't what we needed," he concluded simply. The quiet finality of that statement echoed in the cavernous space, leaving me with a hollow ache that radiated from my chest outward.
